text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein through a peritoneum window, guided by the topography of the inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid damage to the autonomic nerves at the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, making the separation of the mesocolon from the retroperitoneum challenging.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the inferior mesenteric vein to open a peritoneum window, aiming to clearly identify the space between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum.

In a right view of a fresh cadaver, the surgeon applies anterior traction on the inferior mesenteric artery, visibly tensing the fragile sympathetic fibers and nerves around the artery, including the inferior mesenteric plexus and superior hypogastric plexus.

In the view of a fixed cadaver, the surgeon demonstrates a larger peritoneal window under the inferior mesenteric vein. In the second patient, the dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein is initiated as the first step in colonic mobilization.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum under the inferior mesenteric vein at the duodenum-jejunal junction, then performs a planned dissection to separate the space between the mesocolon and prurine alpha tissue. Subsequently, a peritoneal window is opened under the inferior mesenteric artery without dissecting the superior hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon continues the procedure by dividing the inferior mesenteric artery, following the prior dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein. This step is performed to ensure a safe dissection, protecting retroperitoneal structures and avoiding traction injuries.

We consider that the use of inferior mesenteric vein dissection as the first step of colonic mobilization is an anatomical constant landmark more efficient and quicker for left colon laparoscopic procedures.
